The 2025 paper extended the 2024 results to two-dimensional and three-dimensional periodic spaces, and then combined them with existing fluid limit theories, creating the first complete chain: Newtonian hard sphere system → Boltzmann equation → compressible Euler equation / incompressible Navier–Stokes–Fourier equation. Therefore, the simplest understanding is: in 2024, they lengthened the bridge from "microscopic to mesoscopic"; in 2025, they connected the entire bridge from "microscopic to mesoscopic to macroscopic". Of course, the boundaries must be clearly stated: they are solving the classic version of Hilbert's sixth problem, "deriving the fluid equations from Newtonian mechanics, through Boltzmann theory," rather than axiomatizing all of physics. It has a definite scope of application—rare gases, hard sphere models, specific scaling, and the existence of regular solutions—far removed from complex liquids, long-range interactions, chemical reactions, and real boundary conditions. And I believe that "clearly stating the boundaries" is itself extremely important. This is precisely the biggest difference between mathematics and many AI narratives: a truly credible model not only tells you in what conditions it holds true, but also honestly tells you in what conditions it does not. We will return to this point later. The essence of world models is not about remembering more, but about knowing how to forget. The reason I feel Deng Yu's team's work is so relevant to AI is because it brings to the forefront a fact we often underestimate: The world often requires completely different representations at different scales. Moving from particles to fluids is not as simple as scaling up a particle model a trillion times. At the macroscopic scale, even the variables describing the world change: position and velocity at the microscopic level, probability distribution at the mesoscopic level, and density, flow rate, and temperature at the macroscopic level. Moving to the next scale is not about increasing quantity, but about changing the language. Today, when people talk about world models, they often subconsciously understand them as an ever-expanding end-to-end simulator: more data, larger parameters, longer videos, farther rollouts, more agents—as if the underlying simulation is detailed enough, the higher-level patterns will automatically emerge, and we can automatically know how to use it. Hilbert's sixth problem tells us: this step is never free. A truly useful world model, besides making predictions, must also find the **"sufficient states"** of the world at a certain scale: this set of states must be simple enough that it doesn't need to record all the details; and complete enough that it can independently determine what will happen next. Density, velocity, and temperature are a set of sufficient states in the fluid world—they discard almost all molecular-level details, yet can still describe a gust of wind, a stream of water, or even a shock wave in the air. So the real brilliance of world models may not lie in remembering more, quite the opposite—it lies in knowing what can be safely forgotten and proving that forgetting it won't lead to mistakes. The word "safe" is key. Models not only need to compress information, but also must know whether those compressed variables will return to change the outcome through feedback, correlation, or long-term evolution. This, ultimately, brings us back to closure. To summarize the difficulty of this layer with a few sharper questions: Which underlying differences are actually irrelevant? Which relationships must be preserved? Which micro-perturbations will be averaged out? Which micro-correlations will be amplified into macro-structures? And most importantly—is the set of high-level variables we retain sufficient to independently determine the future? If the answer is "yes," then we have obtained a true macro-world model. If we have to go back and check the complete state of each molecule at every step, then what we have is not a theory, but merely a micro-simulator wrapped in a skin. The following sections are the key points I want to discuss: this "cross-scale" problem appears exactly in some of the hottest AI fields today. AI for Drug Discovery: From combining a target to curing a person, how many layers are in between? Drug development is the most typical example. Today's AI can already perform quite well on many single-point problems: predicting protein structure, predicting the binding of molecules and targets, generating candidate compounds, estimating the effect of a gene perturbation on cell expression, and predicting the response of a certain cell to a drug. These are all important. However, the ultimate value of a drug is not determined at the molecular level, but rather in the patient. There lies a very long chain of steps: Molecules → Target Binding → Pathway Influence → Cellular Alteration → Tissue Remodeling → Organ Action → Patient Therapy and Side Effects. A molecule's ability to bind firmly to a protein doesn't guarantee it will alter cell fate as expected. Altering cell fate doesn't mean it can repair tissue. Even if effective in the target organ, it doesn't guarantee it won't be toxic in another. With each layer up the chain, new variables and feedback emerge. Knocking out a gene might compensate through another pathway; inhibiting a protein might cause cells to simply change their metabolic pathway; killing some tumor cells might actually create selective pressure on the remaining cells. Biological systems are rarely linear. Therefore, the most difficult aspect of drug development is often not predicting more accurately at a particular level, but rather: how to reliably transmit interventions at one scale to higher scales. I first heard this concept from Professor Feng Xiqiao, my country's first academician in cell dynamics, and this is precisely the true significance of today's virtual cell research. The goal of AI Virtual Cell is not simply to reconstruct a cell atlas, but to learn the dynamic responses of cells under different conditions and perturbations—researchers have explicitly stated that a unified representation needs to be established across measurement methods and scales, and perturbation predictions should be used to validate the model. However, please note: even if we truly possess a very good virtual cell, we are still far from a virtual patient. Because in the eyes of a tissue model, a cell reverts to its underlying "particle" state. Cells communicate, compete, migrate, and change each other's states. No matter how well a cell model is made, piling up a million such models will not automatically yield a reliable tissue. A macroscopic level is often just a microscopic level below. Life is not a one-off micro-to-macro, but a series of ascending scales. Each step forward presents a completely new Hilbert's sixth problem. Agent Society: Simulating an individual and simulating a society are two different capabilities. Another highly typical direction is Agent Society. A number of companies, including Aaru and Simile, along with a wealth of academic work, are attempting to simulate consumer, voter, corporate, policy, and social behavior using a large number of generative agents. Aaru defines itself as a multi-agent population simulation system; Simile proposes to expand step by step from individuals, long-term journeys, and interactions to the entire market and even society. The academic community has also been able to build an agent for each of over a thousand real participants based on in-depth interviews and test their ability to reproduce the participants' attitudes and behaviors. The first step in this type of work is to make each individual agent sufficiently like the person it represents—can it reproduce a person's preferences, experiences, speaking style, and decision-making habits? Doing this step well is already very valuable. But there's a huge logical leap between "one person" and "a group of people": "If we can accurately simulate every individual, then putting enough individuals together will naturally allow us to accurately simulate society." This sounds self-evident, but it's almost identically flawed to "knowing the Newtonian motion of every particle means knowing the motion of fluids." Suppose a model can very accurately answer "Would you buy this product?", but adding up 100,000 such answers only yields a faster, cheaper market research report. It's not a market model. Because the market is never the sum of a hundred thousand independent answers. It also depends on: whether consumers will change their minds after seeing others buy; whether competitors will lower prices; who the platform algorithm allocates traffic to; how the influence of KOLs and friends spreads; how scarcity changes attractiveness; and—whether the original predictions still hold true after companies act based on the predictions themselves. A influences B, B influences C, and C, in turn, influences A. These feedbacks and correlations are the most crucial part of a social system. It's full of "collisions" and "re-collisions," just like in a gaseous environment. Therefore, the real challenge for Agent Society is never whether "a single agent resembles a person," but rather: When countless agents begin to influence each other, does the entire system still resemble a real society? To evaluate this type of company, I think it should be broken down into at least four layers, and these four layers are progressive: First layer, individual effectiveness: Does the agent resemble the person it represents? Second layer, interaction effectiveness: Does the interaction between two agents resemble the interaction between two real people in a real situation? The third layer, group effectiveness: how closely the resulting distribution of opinions, prices, norms, and behaviors after extensive interaction approximates the real market and society. The fourth layer, intervention effectiveness: whether the model can predict how the real world will change when we change prices, policies, products, or communication structures. The fourth layer has truly enormous commercial value. However, the vast majority of benchmarks remain at the first layer, or at most the second. This precisely exposes a very common misconception in AI investment: the model's benchmark occurs at a lower level, while commercial value occurs at a higher level, and the greatest risk lies between these two levels. I previously discussed grokking with Liu Ziming. He described his research problem as a kind of "AI version of Hilbert's sixth problem," and I think this analogy is very accurate. Grokking is a highly counterintuitive phenomenon: a model might memorize the training set perfectly very early on, but still perform poorly on the test set; after a very long period of training, it suddenly shifts from "memorizing" to "understanding," and its generalization ability dramatically improves. Liu Ziming and his colleagues' research approach was to first understand the internal mechanisms—the dynamics of representation learning and phase diagrams—in very small, very simple models, and then study how these mechanisms extend to larger models as the data scale, parameter scale, regularization, and training conditions change. This is certainly not the same as the particle-to-fluid transition, but it is structurally highly similar. Because neural networks also have different scales: At the micro level, it's about how each parameter changes, how each gradient is updated, and how each neuron and feature is formed. At a higher level, it's about whether the model has formed structured representations, whether it has shifted from memorization to generalization, whether it suddenly starts groking, and whether it exhibits emergent ability after its scale crosses a certain threshold. Today, we're used to using scaling laws to describe macroscopic results: more parameters, more data, more computing power, and the loss drops according to a certain pattern. But scaling laws mainly tell us "how the results change," not "what actually happens internally." It's somewhat like this: we've observed how temperature and pressure change, but we haven't been able to truly deduce them from molecular collisions. Therefore, AI's own Hilbert's sixth problem might be phrased as follows: Can we explain, starting from parameters, gradients, and training dynamics, why macroscopic capabilities like representation, generalization, inference, and emergence emerge? And can we determine—when can the mechanisms observed in small models be extrapolated to large models, and when are they merely illusions at a finite scale? This is very important because there's a very common methodology in AI research: first, discover a phenomenon in a toy model, give a nice explanation, and then assume it holds true in models with hundreds of billions or trillions of parameters. But scaling from a small model to a large model is itself a scale shift—it needs to be proven, not just through analogy. The reverse is also true: observing a nice empirical curve in a large model doesn't mean we understand the underlying mechanism. Just like seeing fluid flowing doesn't mean you've derived Navier-Stokes from Newtonian mechanics. Weather, robots, and organizations are all hitting the same wall. The same problem appears in a whole host of other areas. Weather and Turbulence. It's impossible to analyze every tiny eddy, so processes smaller than the grid scale must be compressed into a closure model. Machine learning is being used to learn how these subgrid processes feed back into large-scale flow fields—but a closure that fits beautifully on training data can quickly become unstable in long-term physical simulations, even violating energy conservation, symmetry, and generalization under extreme conditions. Autonomous Driving and Robotics. Predicting the next frame of a video is not the same as understanding a long-term, interactive physical world. From pixels to objects, from objects to scenes, from scenes to the intentions of other participants, and from single-vehicle behavior to urban traffic, it's a series of continuous scale transitions. A single-vehicle model might be accurate at every step, but when placed in a system composed of numerous human drivers, pedestrians, and other autonomous vehicles, the macroscopic results can be completely different. AI in Enterprises. We often hear a deduction about ROI: if each programmer's efficiency increases by 30%, then the company's R&D efficiency will increase by 30%. But organizational output is never a simple sum of individual outputs. Writing more code may increase the review burden; faster requirements may exacerbate prioritization chaos; easier communication among individuals may lead to more meetings; improvements in local efficiency can be completely wiped out by organizational coordination costs. This is the same kind of error as "smarter agents necessarily form a smarter agent society." From individual capabilities to system output, there are organizational structures, workflows, incentives, information flows, and accountability mechanisms in between. At this point, it's worth reiterating that core judgment: Scaling a simulator is not the same as simulating a scale. Making the simulator larger doesn't mean you've entered a new scale. A million particles are not a fluid model. A million cells are not a human body model. A million consumer agents are not a social model. Increased scale may bring new patterns, but it won't automatically tell you what those patterns are, much less prove their reliability. Every world model has its own Hilbert's Sixth Problem. The significance of Hilbert's Sixth Problem lies not only in its existence for 125 years, nor simply in the fact that someone finally took a giant leap forward. It lies in its profoundly insightful worldview: The world is not a plane with a single resolution, but rather composed of layers of different scales. At different scales, the important objects differ, the descriptive variables differ, the effective laws differ, and even the effective boundaries differ. Microscopic laws do not automatically become macroscopic laws simply by scaling up the system—a genuine theoretical leap is required, necessitating new representations, new assumptions, and a clear understanding of which details can be discarded and which correlations must be retained. Today, we are using AI to simulate more and more worlds: molecules, cells, materials, weather, robots, human behavior, markets and societies, and even AI models themselves. But each direction, in the end, will likely encounter the same question: Can our success at one scale be carried over to the next? If we cannot prove this, we may only possess a very strong local predictor, rather than a true model of the world. We might be able to predict a protein with remarkable accuracy, yet still not know why a patient recovers; we might be able to simulate a consumer with remarkable accuracy, yet still not know why trends form; we might be able to clearly understand how a small model groks, yet still not know why intelligence emerges on a larger scale. A larger model does not equal a larger world. A true world model is not about cramming all the details into an infinitely large neural network until it replicates the entire universe. It needs to know at every scale: what must be remembered, what can be forgotten, what will disappear in the average, what will be amplified in interactions, what can emerge upwards—and why. The ability to translate between these scales is where the truly difficult and valuable step in creating a world model begins.
